review_comment:
If the user thread overflows its own stack, won't it get an MPU violation anyway? I supposed the execption generated will be simpler to comprehend.
I'm OK with this as long as there logic to reprogram the PSPLIM location when going in/out of system calls.
We really need a test case to show that stack overflows during syscalls are caught properly, not sure if there is one.

>> If the user thread overflows its own stack, won't it get an MPU violation anyway? I supposed the execption generated will be simpler to comprehend.

There is no MPU-based protection in this case; stack protection is done via stack-limit checking supported by the built-in feature in ARMv8-M.

>> I'm OK with this as long as there logic to reprogram the PSPLIM location when going in/out of system calls.

Yes there is, this was the main thing done in this PR; ensure PSPLIM is programmed at all times. (I guess that's a step towards functional safety...) Check the diff inside userspace.S.

>> We really need a test case to show that stack overflows during syscalls are caught properly, not sure if there is one.

We have that since Zephyr 2.0; it's inside tests/kernel/fatal
We have that for both MPU-based and SPLIM-based stack-protection.

> We have that since Zephyr 2.0; it's inside tests/kernel/fatal
> We have that for both MPU-based and SPLIM-based stack-protection.

It tests stack overflows for user and supervisor threads..but AFAICT it doesn't cover the scenario where we make a syscall, and the syscall overflows the privilege mode stack.
Nope, it tests for system calls too, I wrote the test for the 2.0 release.
